nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rainforest streams and tributaries with sandy or fine-gravel substrates, leaf litter, submerged roots, and overhanging vegetation; typically soft, slightly acidic, tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: caves and ceramic tubes for spawning (essential)
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
driftwood and bogwood for territory boundaries
dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Amazon sword, Java fern)
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves) to lower pH and provide cover
multiple broken line-of-sight zones to reduce pair aggression toward tankmates

diet.notes: The Green Kribensis accepts a wide range of foods. A high-quality sinking cichlid pellet or micro-pellet works well as a daily staple, and regular offerings of frozen or live foods — bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and white worms — are strongly recommended for conditioning breeding pairs. Blanched vegetables and spirulina wafers are also accepted. The species forages naturally along the substrate, so floating foods are best avoided. Small amounts fed two to three times daily suit it well.

goodWith: small to medium West African tetras (Congo tetra, Phenacogrammus)
small peaceful catfish (dwarf corydoras, Synodontis petricola)
upper-water schooling fish that occupy different tank zones
other non-cichlid mid- to top-water species in larger tanks
avoidWith: other bottom-dwelling cichlids competing for territories

imageCandidates.notes: Udo12345, 4870x2739. Striking sharp pair — foreground male with iridescent green-gold flanks, blue-edged fins and magenta belly, female behind with dark/magenta belly patch and tail eyespots; shows both sexes and the monogamous pair-bond.
Udo12345, 5803x3264. Tack-sharp lone male, full body in frame — iridescent green flanks, magenta belly spot, blue-edged lyretail with eyespots. High resolution, vibrant nuptial colour.
Mummymonkey, 900x600. Sharp female showing the diagnostic vivid magenta belly patch over iridescent green-gold flanks; complements the male shots.

summary: Larger Nigerian relative of the common Krib, reaching ~13 cm with iridescent green flanks and a vivid belly patch in females. Described in 1976, it vanished from the hobby for decades before a trade revival in the 2010s. A devoted cave-spawning monogamous pair-bonder — must never be mixed with P. pulcher or P. taeniatus due to hybridization risk.
careNotes:
The Green Kribensis (Pelvicachromis sacrimontis) is a Nigerian cichlid and the largest member of its genus in common keeping — males can reach 13 cm, noticeably bigger than the familiar common Kribensis (P. pulcher) at around 10 cm. The species has been sporadically available in the hobby for many years, though it remains a specialist fish sought out through dedicated cichlid importers rather than mainstream retail. Multiple wild locality forms are traded, often collectively under the name 'Nigeria Green.'

A 120 L tank with a minimum footprint of 100 cm suits a single bonded pair, but a tank of 200 L or more is advisable if the pair is kept with other fish. Provide multiple caves — half-coconut shells, ceramic tubes, or slate arches — since the pair will claim one as a spawning site and tends to defend the surrounding territory firmly during breeding. Fine sand is the preferred substrate, as the species sifts through it naturally while foraging. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 2–10) mirrors wild conditions and generally encourages breeding, though the species tends to tolerate neutral and moderately hard water outside of breeding periods. Adding tannins via Indian almond leaves or alder cones is beneficial.

The critical husbandry warning: P. sacrimontis hybridizes readily with the common Kribensis (P. pulcher) and with P. taeniatus — these species must never be housed together under any circumstances. Given how rarely pure-line P. sacrimontis is available, hybrid contamination of captive lines is a serious concern among specialist keepers.

Purchase as a proven bonded pair where possible. Established pairs are highly cohesive and splitting a bonded pair is strongly discouraged. During spawning, the pair will chase tankmates away from the cave area; robust upper-water dither fish such as Congo tetras, which occupy a different zone in the water column, can help the pair feel secure enough to spawn. Consistent water quality maintained through regular partial water changes is important to the species’ long-term health.
breedingNotes: The Green Kribensis is a biparental cave-spawner that forms a strong monogamous pair bond. Conditioning the pair with live or frozen foods such as bloodworms and brine shrimp, combined with a slight temperature increase to 26–28°C and very soft, acidic water, generally triggers spawning. The female deposits 50–150 adhesive eggs on the ceiling or walls of the chosen cave, and both parents guard the clutch and fan the eggs with their fins throughout incubation. Eggs typically hatch in around 48–72 hours; the wriggling fry are often moved by the parents to small pits dug in the substrate before the young become free-swimming at around five to seven days after hatching. Both parents remain actively engaged in defense during this period and may herd the fry around the tank for several weeks. From first feeding, the fry accept micro-worms, newly hatched brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), and finely crushed flake. Keeping the pair separate from community tankmates during and after spawning — or setting up a heavily decorated dedicated tank — reduces the risk of escalating territorial aggression. Brood sizes are moderate and generally manageable in the home aquarium. Breeding is considered moderately difficult, owing to the pair-bonding requirement, the species' sensitivity to water chemistry, and the need for appropriate cave furnishings.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pelvicachromis sacrimontis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 13 cm, females smaller), temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 1–15 dH, native range (Nigeria), habitat, breeding behaviour (cave-spawner, biparental), and species incompatibility notes (hybridizes with P. pulcher and P. taeniatus).

additional.name: FishBase — Pelvicachromis sacrimontis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size, native range (Nigeria), original description (Roberts, 1976), and water parameter ranges. Confirmed no synonym conflicts.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Kribensis Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Pelvicachromis care guide covering cave-spawning requirements, pairing behaviour, tank setup recommendations, and diet. Used to cross-check tank size recommendations and feeding guidance applicable to P. sacrimontis given its close relationship to P. pulcher.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Kribensis species profiles
additional.notes: Covers multiple Pelvicachromis species including sacrimontis. Confirmed hybridization risk between P. sacrimontis, P. pulcher, and P. taeniatus; trade revival in 2010s via specialist importers; multiple Nigerian locality forms; larger size than common Krib noted.

additional.name: Cichlid Room Companion — Pelvicachromis sacrimontis
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid reference confirming original description (Roberts 1976), locality data for Nigerian populations, captive breeding notes, and species-level incompatibility with congeners. Supplemented hybridization avoidance guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: The Green Kribensis is a biparental cave-spawner. Both parents guard and fan the clutch of 50–150 eggs; fry become free-swimming around five to seven days after hatching. Soft, acidic water and conditioning with live or frozen foods generally trigger spawning. The pair should be separated from community tankmates during and after breeding.
